Hi @Rosi2143 , thanks for your time reviewing and implementing this! Amazing!

Just a few comments before merging.

1. The `docs/auto_examples` folder is auto generated by a sphinx plugin I've used called sphinx-gallery.  So the original files related to this folder that you need to change are under the folder `tests/examples/`.
2. The changes on `statemachine/callbacks.py` and `statemachine/diagram.py` are not following the automated enforced style guide.  As I'm still working on porting the running automated tests as GH actions on the PRs, I need you to run them locally.

See https://python-statemachine.readthedocs.io/en/develop/contributing.html#get-started to get the reference on how to setup locally.

These are the required steps:

1. Run [poetry](https://python-poetry.org/docs/#installation) ``install`` to install all the dependencies and create a virtual environment:

        poetry install

2. Activate the virtual environment created by poetry:

        poetry shell

2. Install the pre-commit validations on your local git repository copy:

        pre-commit install

2. You can run the pre-commit validations by hand, and the files will be automatically updated by black/ruff:

        pre-commit run --all-files

3. You may need to get a fresh docs build folder, to renew the `auto_examples`, 
    you can safely remove these directories to get a clean state by running:

        rm -rf docs/_build/ docs/auto_examples

4. Run the build again:

        poetry run sphinx-autobuild docs docs/_build/html --re-ignore "auto_examples/.*"

Let me know if you have any trouble with this.

Thanks again for your effort on this!

Best regards!

Beyond the inline change requests (really just a nicer syntax (and faster execution) for shallow-copying dicts):

* I see `pre_login` is a very new feature (https://github.com/pennersr/django-allauth/commit/fde96e6e7da6bbfd9faffaa90d75e6c3e7c2cc41) only released in 0.48.0 (14 hours young at the time of writing) so as-is, this makes django-allauth-2fa incompatible with older versions of django-allauth. Looking at the [test log](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/pull/3692/checks?check_run_id=2383920103) for Django 3.2, it looks like there are a number of deprecation warnings which we need to address (if we don't projects are going to get a lot of warnings that they can't do anything about):

```
RemovedInDjango40Warning: The DEFAULT_HASHING_ALGORITHM transitional setting is deprecated. Support for it and tokens, cookies, sessions, and signatures that use SHA-1 hashing algorithm will be removed in Django 4.0.
```
This is just a test setting which can be safely removed.

```
DeprecationWarning: the imp module is deprecated in favour of importlib; see the module's documentation for alternative uses
```
Looks like we need to switch to importlib.

```
RemovedInDjango40Warning: request.is_ajax() is deprecated. See Django 3.1 release notes for more details about this deprecation.
    if self.request.is_ajax():
```
We're going to have to provide our own implementation of this, as suggested in the Django [release notes](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/3.2/releases/3.1/#id2). 

```
RemovedInDjango40Warning: Passing None for the middleware get_response argument is deprecated.
    super().__init__(get_response)
```
Should be a simple change.

```
RemovedInDjango41Warning: 'oscar.apps.customer' defines default_app_config = 'oscar.apps.customer.apps.CustomerConfig'. However, Django's automatic detection did not find this configuration. You should move the default config class to the apps submodule of your application and, if this module defines several config classes, mark the default one with default = True.
```
Should also be a simple change.

I don't know if you have the time/capacity to address these - if you do then it would be greatly appreciated.

Hi Mike - thanks for checking, I noticed these reviews going by.  I have a change to suggest but it can't go on the diff because it's in bits of the documentation that haven't changed.  There are a bunch of places in the dox that were written in way that is incompatible with the new code, so they should be updated.  For example (going from RTD on https://galsim-developers.github.io/GalSim/_build/html/hsm.html):

- "This routine assumes that (at least locally) the WCS can be approximated as a [PixelScale](https://galsim-developers.github.io/GalSim/_build/html/wcs.html#galsim.PixelScale), with no distortion or non-trivial remapping. Any non-trivial WCS gets completely ignored."  This has to be modified.
- The "HSM output" section gives units that are correct for the current code but not for if you've used the `use_sky_coords` kwarg, since that changes the units - might need to update here.
- The "HSM parameters" section describes default parameter values, some of which do have units if I recall correctly (like pixels of centroid shift).  If i understand correctly, those should still be in pixels even if using `use_sky_coords` because underneath the hood it's still doing the calculations that way and then converting at the end, but it might be worth saying that explicitly?

I might have missed something, so it might be worth a more careful read.

I also wondered if there are any assumptions being made about the WCS, like it being locally affine or something?  Constant across the object?  If so, either would be fine for nearly all reasonable cases, but it would be good to state explicitly.

We're getting really close... hopefully last bunch of comments.

> so looks like there is a version converter issue. But are these models that we could version-convert previously or not? Trying to understand if the error is in the conversion of this op, or if it is an error in the conversion of some other op that we hadn't caught before.

Previously they cannot be version_converted due to missing input shape for Softmax-12 to Softmax-13. As you mentioned, it's possible that there are other version_conversion issues, but for these errors they happened right after the converted Softmax+Reshape.

> The only issue I see with this (softmax) converter is that it requires at least the rank of the input tensor. (This can be improved if we omit the optimization at the beginning for a special case.) Not sure what will happen if the rank is not known. But real models probably have the rank known for all tensors typically, so doubt if this could be the cause.

Good point. I guess that's the issue -- if onnx.shape_inference cannot infer the rank for original Softmax, the new converted Reshape will inherit that rank 0 and cause the shape inference error from the following node due to rank 0.

I think it would be worthwhile adding a code comment here that explains that `c.session.Accept()` blocks until `c.session.Close()` is called, which happens during `c.Close()`, but `c.Close()` also locks `c.m`, so we have to unlock `c.m` while `c.session.Accept()` is running, in order to avoid the deadlock. We could mention that this deadlock was first discovered in go1.19 when https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/409537 started enforcing that listener go routines complete before active connections are cleaned up after a server close / shutdown, and that prior to this, the deadlocks would still have been happening, leaking deadlocked go routines, but functionality would not have been impacted, only performance.

@dashed I see you want this for https://github.com/getsentry/relay/pull/724, but since the code is not used neither here nor in the Relay PR, it is hard to judge whether it actually helps writing what you're trying to write ;)

One suggestion would be to add simple tests here in sentry-python to showcase the usage (I would not expect someone maintaining this repo to have to read Relay tests).

```py
# t = ...
e = Envelope()
e.add_transaction(t)
with pytest.raises(ValueError):
    e.add_transaction(t)  # can only have 1 transaction in the envelope
assert e.get_transaction() is t
```

Since there can only be one transaction, perhaps a better API for the present would be a single property -- with the drawback of getting ugly if we ever allow more than 1 transaction per envelope. If that ever happens we can deprecate the property and add `add_transaction`, perhaps not a big deal.

```py
# t = ...
e = Envelope()
t.transaction = t  # behind the scenes this either adds or replaces an envelope item
assert t.transaction == t
```
Seems sensible :+1:

First of all, so exciting to see you back around here @dmontagu! 🤩🎉

## PR Review

Now to the PR review:

I've been reviewing this for the last couple of days. Very clever all around. Amazing job, as always! 🚀

I added a couple of comments here and there. A couple of opinions in the places where I suspect my opinion could be relevant.

## Type Checks from VS Code - PyRight

I noticed a couple of errors with VS Code/PyRight in some places, I think mainly about getting a dict key form a dict where it is not required, or things like that.

Would it be valuable to point them out and suggest the fixes or is it just too much noise on top of the actual PR review?

I'm just adding a couple of those comments for now.

## Questions

About some of the questions in the description, my opinions inline.

Pretty much in favor with everything, for the rest I don't have a strong opinion or enough knowledge.

**Things left to resolve:**

* [x] Field kwargs
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'examples' kwarg on `Field`?
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Re-add the examples keyword argument
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. All things equal, +1 to re-adding it. Unless there's something against it.
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'const' kwarg on `Field`? (This was used to indicate a field was required and the only allowed value was its default.)
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop this argument in favor of `Literal` and/or validators as appropriate (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [x] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. Even though the Python docs also refer to a Pattern object, but anyway, I personally associate the word "pattern" more easily with the string.
	* [x] Do we want to do a backwards-compatibility handler thing with `**kwargs` — warning about now-invalid kwargs (whether or not we keep them working) on `Field`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Do not attempt to maintain backwards compatibility, but document in the migration guide
			* [@tiangolo] **Strongly** in favor with plan. More details below, and also inline on the PR.


* [x] ConfigDict fields
	* [x] Do we want to re-add 'schema_extra' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* Either way, do we retain/drop support on a config _class_ in the model definition?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.
	* [x] Do we want to re-add 'fields' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of annotations and/or `= Field(...)`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.

* [x] Default values
	* If a default value of a field is not JSON encodable, do we include anything in the schema indicating the default value? Do we error?
	* **Tentative plan:** Just don't include the value in the schema if json encoding fails, but keep the `required` field set appropriately. I believe this is perfectly compatible with JSON schema.
		* [@tiangolo] Soft in favor with plan. I'm not sure about the implications, but I think it should be fine.

* [ ] `__modify_schema__` (-> `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`)
	* Do we retain backwards compatibility with the name `__modify_schema__`, or force migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Force the migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. This is the moment to break changes.
	* Do we support any args/kwargs for `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__` besides the schema itself?
		* Do we pass any info to it from FieldInfo? The CoreSchema? A "source" object? (Could be useful when working with custom generics)
		* **Tentative plan:** Not sure. Would potentially be useful to have access to the core schema and to the type "source" (e.g., to check generic parameter values, etc.)

* [x] FastAPI
	* Do we need to retain functions analogous to any of the following? (Note we have retained `model_schema`, and `schema`.)
		* get_flat_models_from_field/get_flat_models_from_fields
		* get_flat_models_from_model/get_flat_models_from_models
		* get_model_name_map
		* model_process_schema
	* If not, can we drop their tests?
	* **Tentative plan:** Could we instead build a CoreSchema in FastAPI that produces the desired JSON schema?
		* I'm inclined to put significant effort into helping with the migration of FastAPI to pydantic v2, and to implement any necessary analogous functions as a part of that effort. But if it's already clear that we will need them, happy to add them now.
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. And thanks, as always! 🎉 Better not to add premature optimizations/abstractions. And more details in line in the PR.

* [ ] Callable type
	* How should we handle JSON schema generation for fields with type callable? (See, e.g., `tests.test_schema.test_callable_type`)
	* **Tentative plan:** Not sure.

* [ ] Decimal
	* What should the Decimal schema be?
	* **Tentative plan:** Need to come to a consensus; https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/1511 may be relevant
		* Note: although serialization and parsing are different, I think for FastAPI/OpenAPI purposes it is important that the JSON schema be compatible with the serialization type. (Or else generated clients will produce type errors if an API returns a Decimal.) Happy to discuss this more.
			* [@tiangolo] agreed that incompatible clients would be a non-obvious but important problem. Although I'm not sure I understand the problem well and what's the "serialization type" in this context.

## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

## Breaking Changes

About the breaking changes, mostly renaming things, in the small few places this happens, I think this is the right moment to do it and discard the cruft of backwards compatibility for those things.

An improvement in that migration, alternative to keep backwards compatibility, would be a tool to automatically update code to use the new things, for the simple ones (I can imagine, e.g. moving kwargs in `Field()` to `json_schema_extra`, or changing `regex` to `pattern`). Maybe that could also work for the `model_config`.

I haven't done it, but I know it's possible and feasible with LibCST. @Kludex has built a couple of CLI tools that do that type of thing, e.g. https://github.com/Kludex/bump-testclient

This is awesome, really cool work!

--

For raw output, I agree with the decision to simply print the whole BUILD file. Way too tricky to otherwise try to identify which part is applicable. And we hope/expect BUILD files to be fairly small thanks to dep inference. 

However, I could see it getting confusing when you specify multiple targets/files, and mapping what belongs to what. Thoughts on detecting the case of >1 "spec", and logging a warning to consider using the JSON format? We do that with `./pants py-constraints`:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/50b3e525f4a196773d6f3e3e598b5a08bdbb7224/src/python/pants/backend/python/mixed_interpreter_constraints/py_constraints.py#L173-L180

--

The JSON output is excellent: it shows the user what Pants's view of a particular file/target actually is. That transparency is great for debugging. We might want to make JSON the default?

I'd recommend two tweaks:
1) Rather than `alias`, call it `target_type`, or something similar. "Alias" is an implementation detail.
2) I think we want to render default values? That way, we show a complete view of what Pants is doing. It also allows for safer JSON queries, as you're guaranteed the key will exist. If it gets too chatty, we could consider an option like `--ignore-defaults`.

--

In https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/4861, we mentioned adding a table format in a followup. I'm not sure that would really be necessary - the mix of Raw and JSON is very helpful already.

--

In https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/4861, I pondered

> Should [we] evaluate the sources and dependencies fields, or show the raw values?

After seeing this PR, I think no, we should not. `./pants dependencies` already exists for deps, and `./pants filedeps` for sources. It's redundant imo, and also non-trivial, to include those here, including a performance hit.

Thanks! This is fantastic for isolating syncs from one another. I've never played with DB advisory locks, but they seem very powerful for this type of use case -- thanks for the TIL!

I made one note about selecting the partition to lock on, but a more fundamental thought: This looks like it should handle potential conflicts between multiple syncs, but the other side of this I forgot to mention but am remembering now is the need for snapshotting during serialization, where the app models may be getting modified in parallel through the app's API etc, while a serialization task is in progress. So without read-level isolation there, we may pull some records from app models and serialize them, then the app makes changes to both the models we already pulled and some we're about to pull, leading to inconsistencies in what we put into the Store. Is there a special transaction type we could use just for the serialization operation to ensure we're reading from a static snapshot over the course of that serialization, across all the tables we're looping over?

Related to that, and also happening during serialization, was the [incrementing of the current local instance counter](https://github.com/learningequality/morango/blob/7c497ec6401af50bb382902f068bd619af972b04/morango/models/core.py#L194), and ensuring it's an in-place increment rather than a "get and then set" operation that could have race conditions (and lead to two serializations using the same counter etc). I _think_ we may be OK with the current implementation, because it uses an `F` field reference for single-statement incrementing rather than a read/write, but if you think it would be safer, we could add an instance-level lock in there too.

I would argue that this is a sensible work-around for the situation that django-auditlog finds itself in now, an era where there is an actual JSONField in Django instead of the pseudo JSONField from django-jsonfield.

`additional_data` hasn't really been used much in the app I think, so I don't think we should over-optimize by switching to Django's JSONField but treat it as a dumb field to store data in.

Of course alternatively we could switch to [django-jsonfield-backport](https://pypi.org/project/django-jsonfield-backport/) and actually convert all the data from the TextField to an honest JSON field but that would be a pretty big data migration for most installations given the purpose of the app.

So I think rather than renaming the guide. this guide should still be about running multiple agents, and whether you set this this new isolation setting should become one of the decisions you make when doing that - it's now optional instead of required, depending on whether your multiple agents are isolated or not. 

The problem with making this guide just about running *isolated* agents is that i think the only reason somebody would want to do that if they don't want multiple agents is to route requests to specific agents - and we don't allow that currently (much to @slopp 's chargin).

In my head it basically becomes - if you want your multiple agents to be in the same cluster, set the 'replicas' setting or whatever and voila, you have multiple agents. If your agents are isolated (i.e. they can't access each other resources) - then you also need to set this setting in order for features like run termination to work properly

As promised, I played around with this feature and `graphene-federation`. The basics (`@key`) are working if you manually disable the prefix for those types. 
More advanced directives such as `inaccessible` cause problems since their resolving relies on getting the graphql-core type from the schema by `schema.graphql_schema.get_type(entity._meta.name)`. Since `_meta.name` doesn't include the prefix, `get_type` returns `None`, breaking the schema generation.
Other extensions of graphene probably use this functionality as well.

Should we try to attach the prefix to `schema.get_type` in case the first search without the attached prefix fails, or should we let the extensions like `-federation` handle this?

@gabekassel, @ndyck14, there are couple of other configs that I highly recommend to be enabled on a BR  (or router) style device/products:
- For cache table
  -  Suggest increasing `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_TMF_ADDRESS_CACHE_MAX_SNOOP_ENTRIES`  as well (helps allow more of the 128 to be used for snoop optimization)
  - Also the default value for `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_TMF_ADDRESS_QUERY_MAX_RETRY_DELAY`  is rather long (8 hours which I think is given in spec). We typically use much shorter values in internal projects/products.
 

Other feature to enable on routers/BRs:
- `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_SEND_LINK_REQUEST_ON_ADV_TIMEOUT` 
  - This helps make the links between router a lot more reliable).
- I also highly recommend `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_CHILD_SUPERVISION_ENABLE` on router (and on SEDs). 
- And increasing number of message buffers (or heap size if heap is being used for msg buffers).

On SED type devices, I recommend:
- `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_INFORM_PREVIOUS_PARENT_ON_REATTACH`
- `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_ATTACH_BACKOFF_ENABLE` (which is enabled by default).

Optional, only if you feel it has merit: there is a lot of PY2 vs PY3 `#if` branching. Maybe it could be reduced via something like:
```cpp
#if PY_MAJOR_VERSION >= 3
using module_def = PyModuleDef;
#else 
struct module_def {};
#endif
```
Then the code for PY2 code could be mostly identical to PY3 code.
If this is feasible, there will be a universal `create_top_level_module()`. I'd definitely make it public in that case. I'm less certain about making it public if there are different versions for PY2 and PY3, because it will force downstream users to also have `#if`, unless they are certain only PY3 is needed.

Will review the code, but first two quick design questions - cc @jtamiace 

## Icon buttons

It's rare in Kolibri (versus Studio) to use purely icon buttons rather than text-based so I wanted to make sure this was intentional. This is partly because learners on tablets don't have tooltips, and partly because learners are less likely to know iconography conventions in contrast to Studio where we use icon buttons more liberally for "power users".

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/134698606-066257ee-2b83-48f4-bd11-3f3f8a979e59.png)

If we do stick with icon buttons, note that we might be using the wrong one for 'delete':

* https://design-system.learningequality.org/icons/#token-clear
* https://design-system.learningequality.org/icons/#token-delete

## Empty states

We've made 'empty states' more consistent, and I think this design deviates slightly from previous directions:

* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/5051
* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/3550

I would have expected it to look more like:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/134700995-1dd393a3-9368-4a96-a45e-e516bbf0ea02.png)

Perhaps something to track in KDS?

It looks good to me!

I see that you've unified the `read_pagedesc` function by introducing an `isbit` flag, to divide by 1.0 in the usual case, which is okay. I personally would have just had two functions. (It was [Lua's documentation](https://www.lua.org/pil/2.3.html) that convinced me to ignore my instinctive fear of using floating point numbers for integer purposes as long as the values remain in the set of integers or fractions of 2 of integers. And as long as performance is not an issue, which is true here.)

Also on style, you have quite a few one-letter variables, many of them capital letters. There's a style guide (PEP 8?) that says that variables should start with a lower-case, and a one-letter capital technically violates that, though you tend to use them for types and template parameters in compiled languages have traditionally been one-letter capitals.

Just as a question, how well do you control the use of dtypes (i.e. your variables and function arguments named `dtype`)? These can mean a few different things:

   * NumPy `np.dtype` instances, which have the most information (including, for instance, endianness and even units on temporal types) but "`np.dtype`" is only one Python type. The distinctions among integers, floats, booleans, etc. are different _values_ of this type. A NumPy scalar cannot have Python type `np.dtype` (e.g. `isinstance(np.int32(123), np.dtype)` is false).
   * NumPy numeric types, such as `np.int32` and `np.float64`. Each of these is a distinct Python type, and NumPy scalars can have these types (e.g. `isinstance(np.int32(123), np.integer)` is true).
   * Python types, such as `int`, `float`, and `bool`. (`isinstance(np.int32(123), int)` is false.)
   * Strings that represent dtypes, such as `"int32"` or `"i4"` for `np.dtype(np.int32)`, which you don't seem to be using.

The reason I ask is because you're using Python `bool` for the dtype of the boolean column, but this is different from NumPy's boolean type, which is `np.bool_`. They even have different numeric towers: Python's opinion is that booleans are a subtype of integers:

```python
>>> issubclass(bool, int)
True
```

but NumPy thinks that booleans are distinct from integers:

```python
>>> issubclass(np.bool_, np.integer)
False
```

(Python is wrong, by the way. Especially about [truthiness](https://stackoverflow.com/q/39983695/1623645), which I consider to be the worst feature of the Python language because it's not just dynamic typing, which is fine, it's actually _weak_ typing, which is not fine. Weak typing made Perl completely unusable, and that's what sent me to Python in the first place.)

The interchangeability of these things favors hacking away at a data analysis, but it can make a mess of programming in the large, so we just need to be conscious of these distinctions and use them consistently. Personally, I use `np.dtype` as types in some contexts (when it's good for types to be Python values) and `np.number` subclasses in others (when it's good for types to be Python types), and generally don't use the Python types like `bool` or the string representations at all.

I've got nothing against having `get_or_create()` method in the endpoint class.
___
However would you consider to raise custom error instead of `ValueError`? The reason is (as seems to me) that `.get_or_create()` you've implemented calls `.get()` which in it's turn invokes plenty different methods. At least `ValueError` can be raised from [this line](https://github.com/netbox-community/pynetbox/blob/master/pynetbox/core/query.py#L43). I understand we can barely encounter on this cases, but anyway using custom error class can help to differentiate errors to avoid ambiguous.
___
Furthermore, there is `RequestError` in query.py module (at the same directory) which already has [logic to handle 404 NotFound error](https://github.com/netbox-community/pynetbox/blob/master/pynetbox/core/query.py#L43) (at least to persist the context). But `RequestError` accepts a `Request` object in it's `__init__()`. And there is no such object in your `get_or_raise()` method, so you wouldn't raise one from .  
In my opinion original `get()` method should lift up the exception which would be handled by client code. IDK why originally there is [suppressing this exception](https://github.com/netbox-community/pynetbox/blob/master/pynetbox/core/endpoint.py#L183) at all. This is similar to anti-pattern `try: ... except: pass` in fact.
___
My suggestion:
Just create `NotFoundError` at the same module and raise it instead of `ValueError`:
```
class NotFoundError(Exception):
    def __init__(self, name, **kwargs)
        self.message = f"{self.name} not found: {kwargs}"
        super().__init__(self.message)

...

class Endpoint:
    ...

    def get_or_raise(self, *args, **kwargs):
        r"""Queries the DetailsView of a given endpoint but raises an error if no object is returned
        :arg int,optional key: id for the item to be
            retrieved.
        :arg str,optional \**kwargs: Accepts the same keyword args as
            filter(). Any search argument the endpoint accepts can
            be added as a keyword arg.
        :returns: A single :py:class:`.Record` object
        :raises NotFoundError: if kwarg search returns zero or more than one item
        :Examples:
        Referencing with a kwarg that only returns one value.
        >>> nb.dcim.devices.get_or_raise(name='test1-a3-tor1b')
        test1-a3-tor1b
        >>>
        Referencing with an id.
        >>> nb.dcim.devices.get_or_raise(1)
        test1-edge1
        >>>
        """

        resp = self.get(*args, **kwargs)
        if resp is None:
            raise NotFoundError(f"{self.name} not found: {kwargs}")
        return resp

...
```

As for me, it'd be better to remove suppression from [this line](https://github.com/netbox-community/pynetbox/blob/master/pynetbox/core/endpoint.py#L183), but it'd lead to that huge part of scripts using pynetbox face tracebacks with `RequestError` :)

This is a cleaner way to do it.  BaseExceptions should generally not be caught.  The need to catch CancelledError and re-raise it is because it was an `Exception` in 3.7 and promoted to a `BaseExcetpion` only in 3.8.  So, for 3.7 we must make sure it isn't handled with the more general `Exception`.
